evidence: `StartupRuntime` is split into multiple callback bags (`startupInitRuntime`, `startupBootstrapRuntime`, `startupSwapRuntime`) with many nil-guard forwarding methods that add little domain behavior, e.g. `InitializeConfig`, `InitializeAuth`, `InitializeRouter`, `InitializeDocker` in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:109-214`.

`startBackgroundServicesRuntime` continues the same pattern with six embedded callback structs plus parallel `validate()` scans of function pointers (`goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:346-549`), and tests must construct large function matrices (`goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator_test.go:105-211`).

Docker orchestration has overlapping seam layers: `Runtime*Ops` groups in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go:11-94` and separate `dockerRuntime*` groups in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_docker.go:141-204`, both wiring many function fields to mostly direct delegates (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_docker.go:206-290`).

Global config is still pulled directly inside collectors/runtime code (`config.StartramSettingsSnapshot`, `config.GetStartramConfig`, `config.BasePath`) in `goseg/broadcast/collectors/collectors.go:61-74,158`, while large mutable config models (`structs.SysConfig`) remain broad (`goseg/structs/configs.go:9-60`), weakening boundary-focused abstractions.

`startup_orchestrator.go` centralizes multiple phases (init, runtime wiring, background supervision, version discovery, swap/tmp setup) and defines many callback-bag structs plus parallel `validate()` methods, indicating orchestration, dependency assembly, and policy logic are mixed in one file.

`runtime_docker.go` and `runtime_ops.go` both wire large sets of function fields to concrete implementations (`config`, `network`, `click`, docker ops), creating duplicated seam-construction patterns across orchestration layers.

`urbit_operations_runtime.go` contains many transition handlers with near-identical template setup and repeated config-mutate/restart workflows, while `rectify.go` maintains a separate transition reducer map for the same transition domain, increasing coordination overhead.

`session/ws.go` duplicates auth/unauth traversal and mutation logic across broadcast and cleanup paths, while `session/session.go` manages separate global logstream session structures with similar manual container mutation patterns.

Config patch flow mixes recoverable `error` returns with multiple `panic(...)` paths in the same execution surface (e.g., `confPatchFieldValue`, `confPatchSetFieldValue`, `confPatchSetConfigValue`, `buildConfPatchByKey`) in `goseg/config/config_update_schema.go`, while adjacent config persistence paths consistently return wrapped errors in `goseg/config/persistence.go`.

Several operational paths log and continue instead of propagating failure: `StartDockerHealthLoops()` suppresses health-loop startup failure after logging in `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go`, `_ = runtime.mode.setC2cMode(true)` drops mode-switch failure in `goseg/main.go`, and `errorCleanup` only logs rollback failure in `goseg/importer/importer.go`.

Context preservation is inconsistent in workflow/logging paths: multiple branches in `goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` return raw `err` while nearby branches wrap with actionable context, and `splitLogFile` in `goseg/routines/logstream/logs.go` returns a raw open error while later failures are wrapped.

findings.summary: Startup orchestration uses oversized callback-bag abstractions that increase indirection and test setup without adding cohesive policy boundaries.
evidence: `startupInitRuntime` defines 13 function fields and then wraps each with near-identical nil checks and pass-through calls (`goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:109-214`).

`startBackgroundServicesRuntime` embeds six more callback groups and validates each member separately (`goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:346-549`), creating a second callback matrix in the same module.
Tests build large callback literals to satisfy abstraction shape rather than behavior (e.g. `startBackgroundServicesRuntime{...}` in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator_test.go:105-211`).

`main` introduces another wrapper layer (`bootstrapRuntime`, `bootstrapOrchestratorRuntime`, `bootstrapServerRuntime`, `bootstrapConnectivityRuntime`) that largely forwards to orchestrator/server functions (`goseg/main.go:59-111`).
findings.suggestion: Collapse callback bags into a smaller set of behavior-oriented interfaces (for example `SubsystemInitializer`, `BackgroundServiceStarter`, `ServerBootstrapper`) and move optional hooks into explicit option structs so tests override only capabilities they exercise; remove repetitive one-method forwarding wrappers once call sites use those interfaces directly.

findings.summary: Docker runtime seams are duplicated across multiple runtime types, creating parallel abstractions with overlapping contracts.
evidence: `RuntimeContainerOps`/`RuntimeUrbitOps`/`RuntimeConfigOps` etc are defined in `runtime_ops.go:11-94`, while `runtime_docker.go` defines another family (`dockerRuntimeContextOps`, `dockerRuntimeConfigOps`, `dockerRuntimeWireguardOps`, etc.) in `runtime_docker.go:141-204`.

`newDockerRuntime` wires many fields by direct delegation (e.g., `config.BasePath`, `config.Architecture`, `config.DebugMode`, `config.GetWgConf`, `config.SetMinIOPassword`) with little adaptation (`runtime_docker.go:206-290`).

`WireguardRuntime` is a third seam bag with overlapping concerns (`StartContainerFn`, file ops, config access) and additional wrapper callback `WriteWgConfFn func(WireguardRuntime) error` (`runtime_docker.go:30-49,76` and used in `wireguard.go:25-30`).
Tests mirror this complexity with many stub adapters and no-op wrappers to satisfy seam shapes (`runtime_seams_test.go:15-293`).
findings.suggestion: Consolidate to one seam model per use case: keep a single `RuntimeDeps` contract for orchestration flows and compose narrow nested interfaces for file/docker/config concerns; remove duplicate `dockerRuntime*` vs `Runtime*Ops` layers and eliminate self-referential callbacks like `WriteWgConfFn(func(WireguardRuntime) error)` in favor of direct method injection.

findings.summary: Modules that define runtime abstractions still bypass them and read global config snapshots directly, making seams leaky and reducing abstraction honesty.
evidence: `collectorRuntime` injects many dependencies, but `constructPierInfo` still reads globals directly (`config.StartramSettingsSnapshot`, `config.GetStartramConfig`) instead of routing through runtime (`collectors.go:61-74`).
`localBackupsForPeriod` reaches global runtime context (`config.BasePath`) directly within collectors (`collectors.go:158`), further mixing runtime seams with ambient state.

`RectifyRuntime` and `importerRuntime` are built as dependency carriers, yet defaults heavily bind to config singletons (`rectify.go:67-89`, `importer.go:96-115`) and methods fall back to package globals (`rectify.go:92-96`).

Underlying config model remains a broad mutable aggregate (`structs.SysConfig` has many unrelated concerns in `structs/configs.go:9-60`), encouraging snapshot/bag passing instead of bounded domain ports.
findings.suggestion: Enforce seam purity at module boundaries: require all external state reads via injected runtime interfaces, remove direct `config.*` calls from runtime-aware functions, and split `SysConfig`-derived access into domain-specific providers (networking, startram, backup, runtime context) so consumers cannot depend on monolithic config state.

findings.summary: Several local runtime wrappers are thin nil-default layers that add boilerplate indirection without meaningful policy encapsulation.
evidence: `wifiRuntime` + `withWiFiRuntime` manually merge six function pointers (`wifi.go:31-72`), then exported functions call `defaultWiFiService()` wrappers (`wifi.go:312-338`), adding call depth for mostly direct command execution.
`NetworkRuntime` repeats `runtime = runtime.withDefaults()` at the top of each method (`network.go:42,77,97,115,132,155,177`) instead of centralizing defaults at construction.
Tests in adjacent modules frequently build custom runtime structs solely to toggle one behavior, indicating abstraction shape cost (e.g., multiple runtime literals in `main_test.go:131-200`).
findings.suggestion: Prefer constructor-time dependency objects with required fields and small optional options structs; avoid per-call `withDefaults()` and default wrapper fan-out by constructing fully initialized services once and passing them through explicit module entry points.

findings.summary: Orchestration runtime uses oversized callback-bag structs and repeated validation/wiring logic, blurring module boundaries.
evidence: `startup_orchestrator.go` defines multiple runtime structs (`startupInitRuntime`, `startBackground*Services`, `StartupRuntime`) with many function fields, then separately implements `withDefaults`, `validate`, and startup execution paths.
`runtime_core.go` and `runtime_ops.go` repeat similar option/merge patterns (`With*Ops`, `With*Dependencies`, `defaultRuntime*`) for overlapping dependencies.

`runtime_docker.go` redefines another large runtime decomposition (`dockerRuntime*Ops`) and repeats mapping from the same concrete modules (`config`, `network`, docker funcs), creating parallel wiring systems.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a smaller set of cohesive service interfaces per startup phase (e.g., `InitServices`, `BackgroundServices`, `ContainerRuntime`) and consolidate default wiring/validation into shared builders to remove duplicated callback-bag construction.

findings.summary: Urbit operation handlers duplicate transition template and restart/update flow across many functions instead of using a data-driven command model.
evidence: `urbit_operations_runtime.go` repeats `runUrbitTransitionTemplateFn` setup in `toggleChopOnVereUpdate`, `togglePower`, `toggleDevMode`, `toggleNetwork`, `handleLoom`, and `handleSnapTime`, with similar mutate-config then restart-or-not patterns.

Transition semantics are also maintained separately in `rectify.go` via `urbitTransitionReducers`, so adding/changing a transition often requires synchronized edits in two different structural representations.
findings.suggestion: Define a declarative transition command registry (transition type, preconditions, config mutation, restart policy, emitted events) and generate both execution handlers and reducer mappings from that shared model.

findings.summary: Session code mixes transport state, auth-state transitions, and broadcast lifecycle in overlapping manual map/slice mutation paths.
evidence: `ws.go` combines connection registration, auth/unauth bucket transitions, write-failure cleanup, and broadcast behavior in one manager, with duplicated loops in `BroadcastAuth` vs `BroadcastUnauth` and in auth/unauth cleanup passes.
`session.go` separately manages logstream session state with additional global runtime mutation APIs, creating two independent session-state management styles in the same package.
`logger.go` depends on `session.LogstreamRuntime`, coupling logging sink configuration to this mutable global session subsystem.
findings.suggestion: Split session concerns into focused components (`ConnectionRegistry`, `AuthSessionStore`, `Broadcaster`, `LogstreamSessionStore`) and unify shared iteration/filter logic through common helpers to reduce parallel mutation code paths.

findings.summary: Action contract API duplicates namespace-specific helpers and scans that should be namespace-agnostic.
evidence: `actions.go` has parallel Upload/C2C families (`UploadActionContractForAction` vs `C2CActionContractForAction`, metadata/deprecated/active checks, parse/supported wrappers) that mirror control flow with different backing slices.
Tests in `actions_test.go` must cover duplicated namespace-specific surfaces, increasing maintenance burden for each new action namespace.
findings.suggestion: Use a single namespace-indexed action registry that exposes generic lookup/metadata/compatibility functions and keep namespace-specific wrappers minimal or generated.

findings.summary: Config patch handling violates a single failure contract by mixing panic-based aborts with normal error returns.
evidence: `buildConfigPatch` and parse helpers return `error` for invalid input (`unsupported config key`, type mismatches), but helper internals in the same module can `panic` on invalid field/path metadata (`confPatchFieldValue`, `confPatchSetFieldValue`, `confPatchSetConfigValue`, `buildConfPatchByKey`).

Config persistence and load paths in `goseg/config/persistence.go` consistently propagate wrapped errors, so callers of config operations cannot rely on one predictable failure mode across config boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Replace panic paths in config patch helpers with typed errors (or guarded init-time validation that fails fast before serving) and enforce one runtime contract: config update operations should always return wrapped errors to callers.

findings.summary: Critical operational failures are inconsistently downgraded to logs, producing false-success behavior for callers.
evidence: `StartDockerHealthLoops()` logs and suppresses `StartDockerHealthLoopsWithContext` errors (`logger.Errorf(...)` then returns void), so startup callers cannot react to subsystem startup failure.
`C2cCheckWith` ignores `setC2cMode` failure via `_ = runtime.mode.setC2cMode(true)`, unlike adjacent checks that log and return on failure.
`errorCleanup` logs rollback errors (`Import rollback encountered errors`) but has no propagation path, so upstream orchestration cannot distinguish full rollback from partial rollback.
findings.suggestion: Define explicit error-boundary policy for background/orchestration operations: either return errors to a supervising loop/channel or emit structured failure events; avoid log-only suppression for state-changing operations.

findings.summary: Raw error returns in selected paths drop operation context while neighboring code wraps errors, making failures harder to classify.
evidence: In `urbit_operations_runtime.go`, several branches return bare `err` (`togglePower`, `toggleDevMode`, `toggleNetwork`, `handleLoom`, `handleSnapTime`) while adjacent branches use contextual wrapping (e.g., `Failed to get ship status...`, `Couldn't start ...`).
`splitLogFile` returns raw `err` when opening file (`os.Open`) in `logs.go`, while downstream failures in the same function are wrapped with file/chunk context.
This mixed pattern makes upstream logs and error handling inconsistent because some failures carry operation metadata and others do not.
findings.suggestion: Adopt a package-level rule to wrap external/IO boundary errors with operation context before returning; keep raw returns only for already-contextualized domain errors validated with `errors.Is/As`.
